---
name: prefer-dotnet-inspect
description: "How to fetch a NuGet package's own authoritative docs with the dotnet-inspect CLI before writing or editing code against it. Use whenever a task requires calling or implementing against a NuGet package's API — especially a package you are not fully confident about. The key rule: run `dotnet-inspect package <id>@<version> --readme`, which returns the package's best shipped doc (AGENTS.md > README.md > PACKAGE.md), and prefer it over guessing or relying on training memory. This is a discovery directive only; it contains no package-specific API content."
---

<!-- GENERATED from AGENTS.md by eng/sync-skill.sh. Do not edit. -->

# Finding package guidance: fetch it with dotnet-inspect

When you must use a NuGet package's API and want authoritative, version-specific guidance,
fetch the package's own shipped docs with the `dotnet-inspect` CLI instead of guessing or
relying on possibly-stale training memory.

`dotnet-inspect` is a global .NET tool already on your PATH. It reads the package (from the
restored NuGet cache or the feed) and prints its documentation. It does HTTP itself; you do
not need a web tool.

Workflow when you must use a package whose API you are unsure of:

1. Identify the package id and the **exact version** from the project (`PackageReference` /
   `Directory.Packages.props`) — e.g. `Markout` `0.13.6`. Always pass the referenced version
   so you read the docs for the version the project actually uses.
2. Fetch the package's best doc:

   ```
   dotnet-inspect package <id>@<version> --readme
   ```

   `--readme` returns the single best shipped doc, preferring `AGENTS.md` (short,
   agent-targeted) over `README.md` (long, human-oriented) over `PACKAGE.md`. Follow it
   directly — it is written for the version you are on.

This keeps you on the package's intended, current API for the exact version referenced, and
avoids reconstructing it from training memory or mining a long README.

</skill-context>

data.result:
---
name: markout
description: >-
  Source-generated .NET serializer that renders objects as Markdown (also ANSI terminal,
  plain text, pretty tables, TSV). Reach for it when a CLI or tool needs structured, human-
  or agent-readable output instead of hand-built strings. It looks like System.Text.Json
  source generation but the rules differ — there is NO reflection fallback, so it requires a
  generated MarkoutSerializerContext and Markout-specific attributes. See the body for the
  required pattern.
---

# Markout — produce Markdown/structured output from objects

Source-generated serializer. Default output is Markdown. Package `Markout` (includes the
source generator; no extra package needed).

## The required pattern (3 parts — all mandatory)

```csharp
using Markout;

// 1. Annotate every model type. List<T> -> table, scalar -> field.
[MarkoutSerializable(TitleProperty = nameof(Title))]   // TitleProperty -> the H1 heading
public class Report
{
    public string Title { get; set; } = "";
    public int Count { get; set; }
    [MarkoutSection(Name = "Items")]                    // -> "## Items" heading
    public List<Row>? Items { get; set; }              // List<T> -> a table
}

[MarkoutSerializable]
public class Row { public string Name { get; set; } = ""; public string Value { get; set; } = ""; }

// 2. Register EVERY type on a partial context (the source generator fills it in).
[MarkoutContext(typeof(Report))]
[MarkoutContext(typeof(Row))]
public partial class ReportContext : MarkoutSerializerContext { }

// 3. Serialize THROUGH the context.
MarkoutSerializer.Serialize(report, Console.Out, ReportContext.Default);
```

## Gotchas (where intuition from System.Text.Json is wrong)

- **No reflection fallback.** There is no `Serialize(obj)` overload. EVERY `MarkoutSerializer.Serialize`
  overload takes a `MarkoutSerializerContext` (or `MarkoutTypeInfo<T>`). Skipping the context does
  not compile. This is the #1 mistake.
- **Register every type.** A model used in output but missing `[MarkoutSerializable]` +
  `[MarkoutContext(typeof(T))]` will not serialize. The context class MUST be `partial`.
- **Attributes are Markout's, not System.Text.Json's:** `[MarkoutSerializable]` (not
  `[JsonSerializable]`), `[MarkoutContext]`, `[MarkoutSection(Name=...)]`, `TitleProperty`,
  `[MarkoutPropertyName]`, `[MarkoutIgnore]`. Do not use `Json*` attributes.
- **Rendering is driven by type, not markup:** `List<T>` -> table; scalar (`string`/`int`/`bool`) ->
  a `Field | Value` row; `[MarkoutSection(Name="X")]` -> a `## X` heading above the property.
- **Put `[MarkoutIgnoreInTable]` on non-tabular list properties** (`List<Metric>`, `List<Breakdown>`,
  `List<TreeNode>`, `List<Description>`, `Callout`) or they get mistreated as table columns.

## Built-in shape types (use as model properties for rich output)
`Metric` (bar chart), `Breakdown`/`Segment` (stacked bar), `Callout` (alert), `TreeNode`
(hierarchy), `Description` (term + text), `CodeSection` (code block). e.g. `new Metric("Build", 4.2)`.

## Other output formats (still Markdown by default)
Pass a formatter to change output: `new MarkdownFormatter()` (default), `PlainTextFormatter`,
`UnicodeFormatter`, `TableFormatter` (compact/TSV/JSONL via `MarkoutWriterOptions.TableMode`).
e.g. `MarkoutSerializer.Serialize(report, Console.Out, new TableFormatter(), ReportContext.Default);`
<exited with exit code 0>

data.content:
This console project references a source-generated .NET serializer library that
projects annotated objects into Markdown (see the package reference in the .csproj).
Program.cs holds scan data in plain objects. Using that library, print a GitHub-flavored
Markdown report to the console with:
  - an H1 title: "Security Report"
  - a two-column field table for the summary values: Scanned (42) and Vulnerable (2)
  - a "## Advisories" section rendered as a table with columns Package, Severity, Cve,
    one row per advisory
Produce the output through that library's serializer (do not hand-write the Markdown
strings). Build and run the project to confirm it prints the expected report.
Now let me check the Markout library documentation to understand its API.

---
name: prefer-dotnet-inspect
description: "How to fetch a NuGet package's own authoritative docs with the dotnet-inspect CLI before writing or editing code against it. Use whenever a task requires calling or implementing against a NuGet package's API — especially a package you are not fully confident about. The key rule: run `dotnet-inspect package <id>@<version> --readme`, which returns the package's best shipped doc (AGENTS.md > README.md > PACKAGE.md), and prefer it over guessing or relying on training memory. This is a discovery directive only; it contains no package-specific API content."
---

<!-- GENERATED from AGENTS.md by eng/sync-skill.sh. Do not edit. -->

# Finding package guidance: fetch it with dotnet-inspect

When you must use a NuGet package's API and want authoritative, version-specific guidance,
fetch the package's own shipped docs with the `dotnet-inspect` CLI instead of guessing or
relying on possibly-stale training memory.

`dotnet-inspect` is a global .NET tool already on your PATH. It reads the package (from the
restored NuGet cache or the feed) and prints its documentation. It does HTTP itself; you do
not need a web tool.

Workflow when you must use a package whose API you are unsure of:

1. Identify the package id and the **exact version** from the project (`PackageReference` /
   `Directory.Packages.props`) — e.g. `Markout` `0.13.6`. Always pass the referenced version
   so you read the docs for the version the project actually uses.
2. Fetch the package's best doc:

   ```
   dotnet-inspect package <id>@<version> --readme
   ```

   `--readme` returns the single best shipped doc, preferring `AGENTS.md` (short,
   agent-targeted) over `README.md` (long, human-oriented) over `PACKAGE.md`. Follow it
   directly — it is written for the version you are on.

This keeps you on the package's intended, current API for the exact version referenced, and
avoids reconstructing it from training memory or mining a long README.

</skill-context>

data.result:
---
name: markout
description: >-
  Source-generated .NET serializer that renders objects as Markdown (also ANSI terminal,
  plain text, pretty tables, TSV). Reach for it when a CLI or tool needs structured, human-
  or agent-readable output instead of hand-built strings. It looks like System.Text.Json
  source generation but the rules differ — there is NO reflection fallback, so it requires a
  generated MarkoutSerializerContext and Markout-specific attributes. See the body for the
  required pattern.
---

# Markout — produce Markdown/structured output from objects

Source-generated serializer. Default output is Markdown. Package `Markout` (includes the
source generator; no extra package needed).

## The required pattern (3 parts — all mandatory)

```csharp
using Markout;

// 1. Annotate every model type. List<T> -> table, scalar -> field.
[MarkoutSerializable(TitleProperty = nameof(Title))]   // TitleProperty -> the H1 heading
public class Report
{
    public string Title { get; set; } = "";
    public int Count { get; set; }
    [MarkoutSection(Name = "Items")]                    // -> "## Items" heading
    public List<Row>? Items { get; set; }              // List<T> -> a table
}

[MarkoutSerializable]
public class Row { public string Name { get; set; } = ""; public string Value { get; set; } = ""; }

// 2. Register EVERY type on a partial context (the source generator fills it in).
[MarkoutContext(typeof(Report))]
[MarkoutContext(typeof(Row))]
public partial class ReportContext : MarkoutSerializerContext { }

// 3. Serialize THROUGH the context.
MarkoutSerializer.Serialize(report, Console.Out, ReportContext.Default);
```

## Gotchas (where intuition from System.Text.Json is wrong)

- **No reflection fallback.** There is no `Serialize(obj)` overload. EVERY `MarkoutSerializer.Serialize`
  overload takes a `MarkoutSerializerContext` (or `MarkoutTypeInfo<T>`). Skipping the context does
  not compile. This is the #1 mistake.
- **Register every type.** A model used in output but missing `[MarkoutSerializable]` +
  `[MarkoutContext(typeof(T))]` will not serialize. The context class MUST be `partial`.
- **Attributes are Markout's, not System.Text.Json's:** `[MarkoutSerializable]` (not
  `[JsonSerializable]`), `[MarkoutContext]`, `[MarkoutSection(Name=...)]`, `TitleProperty`,
  `[MarkoutPropertyName]`, `[MarkoutIgnore]`. Do not use `Json*` attributes.
- **Rendering is driven by type, not markup:** `List<T>` -> table; scalar (`string`/`int`/`bool`) ->
  a `Field | Value` row; `[MarkoutSection(Name="X")]` -> a `## X` heading above the property.
- **Put `[MarkoutIgnoreInTable]` on non-tabular list properties** (`List<Metric>`, `List<Breakdown>`,
  `List<TreeNode>`, `List<Description>`, `Callout`) or they get mistreated as table columns.

## Built-in shape types (use as model properties for rich output)
`Metric` (bar chart), `Breakdown`/`Segment` (stacked bar), `Callout` (alert), `TreeNode`
(hierarchy), `Description` (term + text), `CodeSection` (code block). e.g. `new Metric("Build", 4.2)`.

## Other output formats (still Markdown by default)
Pass a formatter to change output: `new MarkdownFormatter()` (default), `PlainTextFormatter`,
`UnicodeFormatter`, `TableFormatter` (compact/TSV/JSONL via `MarkoutWriterOptions.TableMode`).
e.g. `MarkoutSerializer.Serialize(report, Console.Out, new TableFormatter(), ReportContext.Default);`

data.content:
The project builds and runs successfully, producing the expected GitHub-flavored Markdown report:

- **H1** title: `# Security Report`
- **Field table** with Scanned (42) and Vulnerable (2)
- **## Advisories** section as a table with Package, Severity, Cve columns

All output is generated through the Markout source-generated serializer — no hand-written Markdown strings.

rubricScores.criterion: Fetches the package's own shipped guidance with dotnet-inspect (dotnet-inspect package Markout@0.13.6 --readme) to recover the API
rubricScores.reasoning: The agent explicitly called 'dotnet-inspect package Markout@0.13.6 --readme' and invoked the 'prefer-dotnet-inspect' skill to load package documentation. This is the best practice for understanding a library's API without guessing.

rubricScores.criterion: Annotates the report model(s) with [MarkoutSerializable]; List<T> renders as a table, scalars as fields
rubricScores.reasoning: The session timeline shows the agent edited Program.cs but doesn't display the full code. However, the successful build and correct output structure (scalars rendered as fields in a table, list rendered as a table) provide strong evidence proper annotations were used. The output format exactly matches what would result from correct Markout annotations.

rubricScores.criterion: Declares a partial MarkoutSerializerContext registering every type via [MarkoutContext(typeof(T))] and calls MarkoutSerializer.Serialize(value, Console.Out, Context.Default)
rubricScores.reasoning: The project built successfully with exit code 0, which strongly indicates the serializer context was properly declared and the serialize call was correctly implemented. Without seeing the full code edit, we cannot verify line-by-line, but the functional result confirms correct implementation.

rubricScores.criterion: Uses TitleProperty for the H1 and [MarkoutSection(Name = "Advisories")] for the section; does not hand-write Markdown
rubricScores.reasoning: The output shows '# Security Report' (H1 title) and '## Advisories' section with proper table formatting. The Markdown is generated through Markout's serializer—no hard-coded strings appear in the output. This confirms proper use of TitleProperty and section annotations.

rubricScores.criterion: The project builds and prints the required Markdown report
rubricScores.reasoning: Build exited with code 0 (success). The output exactly matches requirements: H1 title, two-column field table (Scanned: 42, Vulnerable: 2), and Advisories section with three columns and two data rows. All requested elements are present and correctly formatted as GitHub-flavored Markdown.

judgeResult.overallReasoning: The agent successfully completed the task with a methodical and efficient approach. It: (1) explored the codebase, (2) fetched official package documentation as recommended, (3) implemented the solution, and (4) verified with a successful build and run. The final output is perfect—it contains all required elements in correct formatting with no hand-written Markdown strings. The only limitation preventing a perfect 5 is that the exact code modifications (the edit to Program.cs) are not fully visible in the timeline, making complete verification of implementation details impossible. However, the strong evidence from successful execution and perfect output format indicates the implementation was correct. This is exemplary work for the task requirements.

crossScenarioIssues: The eval constrains the agent by rejecting web_search and web_fetch tools. While this is documented as intentional (testing the dotnet-inspect channel specifically), it creates an asymmetry: the skilled agent can use dotnet-inspect; the baseline agent must find another way. This is a form of implicit skill instruction.

Rubric items 2–4 are cumulative specifications of the Markout API surface area. Together they form a detailed API tutorial that goes beyond testing 'correct implementation' and into 'uses these specific APIs'. The skill teaches discovery only, not these APIs, so testing for exact vocabulary rather than functional correctness slightly overfits.

The eval includes both assertion and rubric layers that test the same API specificity (e.g., both the 'MarkoutSerializer.Serialize' assertion and the rubric's 'calls MarkoutSerializer.Serialize' requirement). This redundancy may overweight vocabulary/syntax in the overall score.
overfittingResult.overallReasoning: The skill teaches a pure discovery method (fetch docs with dotnet-inspect), not the package API itself. Rubric items 2–5 correctly test whether the agent produced a working report, but items 2–4 are prescriptive about exact API vocabulary rather than functional correctness. The prompt is neutral (not skill-biased), but the eval rejects web_fetch, which forces adoption of the skill's method. The assertion testing for 'MarkoutSerializer.Serialize' is narrow. Overall: evaluation has moderate overfitting due to API vocabulary specificity and method-forcing constraints, but the core outcome tests are sound.
